title: Why Cultural Awareness is Essential for Global AI
gist: A Cohere-conducted survey of 81 users across 22+ countries found that 89.5% of non-English primary speakers switch to English to get better AI results, over a third feel AI does not adequately understand their cultures, and 67% fear AI will further marginalize their culture — making cultural awareness a product gap with economic equity implications.

insights.claim: Among non-English primary speakers using AI, 89.5% reported switching to English because responses in their native language were perceived as lower quality — demonstrating that multilingualism without cultural grounding degrades utility for the majority of the world's population.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: AI vendors that solve cultural-linguistic parity, not just language translation, will unlock a much larger total addressable market; current leaders risk losing non-English users.
insights.quote: 89.5% reported switching from their primary language when interacting with AI. The main reason for switching was the perceived higher quality and accuracy of responses, with English being the most commonly adopted alternative language.

insights.claim: AI systems currently default to culturally dominant subgroups within languages — e.g., associating French with France over other Francophone regions, or responding in Bengali when prompted in Assamese — indicating the cultural gap compounds language support.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: Model evaluation suites that test only language accuracy miss a systematic bias that reduces product value for billions of non-dominant-culture users.
insights.quote: AI systems often default to culturally dominant groups within languages (e.g., associating French primarily with France over other Francophone regions), context-switch to neighboring higher resource languages or regions

insights.claim: Over one-third (38%) of surveyed AI users globally rate AI's cultural understanding below 5 out of 10, with failures concentrated in creative writing, translation, local information lookup, legal/government tasks, and medical questions.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: High-value verticals — legal, medical, government — remain poorly served for non-Western users, representing a product opportunity for culturally aware specialized AI.
insights.quote: Over one-third of respondents (38%) felt that AI does not adequately understand their cultures, giving a score of <5 out of 10.

insights.claim: 63% of surveyed AI users reported their cultural norms were violated at least once when interacting with AI, including failures in formality calibration, historical narrative perspective, and code-switched language handling.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: Cultural norm violations are not edge cases but a majority user experience, creating trust and adoption risk for AI products in non-Western markets.
insights.quote: The majority of participants (63%) indicated that their cultural norms have been violated at least once when interacting with AI.

insights.claim: Users prompting AI in Egyptian Arabic, Korean, Japanese, Italian, Croatian, and Hindi consistently reported AI presenting Western narratives of historical events while overlooking their culture's practices, traditions, and perspectives.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: Historical and political content generation is a liability area for AI companies in non-Western markets; regulatory and reputational risk is underpriced.
insights.quote: Multiple participants reported that while using AI models in Egyptian Arabic/Korean/Japanese/Italian/Croatian or Hindi, AI frequently presented Western narratives of historical events while overlooking the practices, traditions, and perspectives of their respective cultures.

insights.claim: Because AI functions significantly better for English-speaking Western-context users, the productivity gains of the AI era are disproportionately flowing to already-privileged populations, compounding existing economic inequalities.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: Policy risk and ESG scrutiny on AI equity could accelerate regulation in non-Western markets; investors in AI beneficiaries should monitor geopolitical blowback.
insights.quote: If AI works significantly better for English-speaking Western-context users, then the productivity gains of the AI era will flow disproportionately to populations that are already privileged.
insights.locator: Why This Matters in Real World Scenarios

insights.claim: The cultural gap is self-reinforcing: users who receive poor AI results use AI tools less, generating fewer data points from those communities, which means models improve even less over time for those users — a compounding feedback loop.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: First movers who deliberately invest in culturally diverse training data have a structural long-term moat; latecomers face a compounding data disadvantage.
insights.quote: users who receive poor results are less likely to keep using the AI tools which means less usage data from these communities flow back into the model development cycle—and without explicit interventions, models improve even less over time
insights.locator: Why This Matters in Real World Scenarios

insights.claim: 67% of surveyed users expressed moderate to strong concern that AI will further marginalize their culture in the future or reduce it to stereotypes, indicating a latent trust deficit with commercial implications.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: Cultural trust deficit will hinder AI adoption in emerging markets; vendors with credible cultural-awareness roadmaps could convert this anxiety into competitive differentiation.
insights.quote: 67% expressed moderate to strong agreement that AI would further marginalize their culture in the future or reduce it to stereotypes (scoring 3 and above on a 1-5 scale).

themes.theme: Cross-Cultural AI Use Cases and Misinformation Risk

insights.claim: 63% of respondents use AI to understand cultures other than their own, making accurate multicultural representation critically important for preventing cross-cultural misinformation at scale.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: AI-driven cross-cultural misrepresentation is an underappreciated misinformation vector; media and journalism verticals should scrutinize AI cultural accuracy before deployment.
insights.quote: 63% of respondents indicated a 3 or more out 5 when asked if they use AI to understand cultures apart from their own. If users are using AI to gain cross-cultural understanding, it is even more important that cultures are adequately represented
insights.locator: Why This Matters in Real World Scenarios

insights.claim: Culturally aware AI requires region-specific localization beyond language translation, formality calibration, diverse historical source citation, natural code-switched language handling, and guidance reflecting users' social and legal context.
insights.speaker: Cohere
insights.so_what: These requirements define a new dimension of model evaluation and fine-tuning investment; benchmark providers that add cultural competency tests will gain strategic relevance.
insights.locator: What should cultural-aware AI look like?
